questions.expected_answer: Structural pattern: declaring the measurement protocol (timing and basis) structures the uncertainty; undeclared measurement produces maximal uncertainty as compensation for the unknown. Primary domain equivalent: compile-time proof vs runtime measurement in clock-aware programming. Bridge: quantum uncertainty is not a fixed quantity — it is a function of the measurement declaration. When the measurement protocol is pre-declared (interferometry, atomic clock), the uncertainty is structured by the declaration and the system is prepared accordingly. When the measurement is undeclared, the uncertainty relation gives the worst-case bound. In the primary domain: a circuit's execution time is not a fixed quantity — it is a function of the declaration. When budget_ticks is declared and verified against the hardware latency table, the execution time is structurally bounded by the declaration. When execution time is not declared, the profiler measures it at runtime and produces a sample from a distribution (the worst-case equivalent of the uncertainty bound). The compile-time proof structures the execution time; the runtime measurement samples from the undeclared distribution. Declared measurement protocol : compile-time proof :: undeclared measurement : runtime profiling.

discriminator.0_to_1: Must identify 'compile-time proof vs runtime measurement' as the primary equivalent.
discriminator.1_to_2: Must explain: undeclared measurement → uncertainty relation gives worst-case bound ↔ undeclared execution time → profiler samples from unknown distribution; declared measurement protocol → structured uncertainty ↔ declared budget_ticks → structurally bounded execution.

questions.cross_domain_mapping: Operations research — Queuing theory / Little's Law
questions.question: A queuing theory textbook introduces Little's Law: "L = λW states that the mean number of items in a system equals the arrival rate multiplied by the mean time each item spends in the system. Variance in W — the sojourn time — produces variance in L, and high variance produces tail latency. The fundamental source of variance in W is variance in the arrival process and the service time distribution. In a D/D/1 queue — deterministic arrivals, deterministic service, single server — there is no queue: L = 0 between arrivals, W = service time exactly. The queue, and the tail latency it produces, exists only when arrivals or service times are random. Declaring both eliminates the queue by construction." What primary domain mechanism is this structurally identical to, and why?
questions.expected_answer: Structural pattern: variance in timing (arrival and service) produces queuing and tail latency; declaring both arrival timing and service time eliminates the queue by construction. Primary domain equivalent: budget_ticks + admission test eliminating execution variance. Bridge: the D/D/1 queue has L = 0 because arrivals and service times are declared — no item waits because every arrival is expected and every service window is reserved. Variance in arrival or service produces the queue (and tail latency). In the primary domain: budget_ticks declares the service time of each circuit (the time it takes to complete). The dispatch table declares the arrival time (when the circuit begins). The admission test verifies that no circuit's budget overflows into the next circuit's window. The result is a D/D/1 system: declared arrival, declared service, no queue. Tail latency (p99.9 in conventional systems) arises from queue variance — random arrivals to the OS scheduler, random service times from undeclared memory access. Declared timing eliminates both variance sources. Little's Law still holds: L = λW, but W is declared and constant, so L = 0. The benchmark claim 'OS jitter eliminated' is exactly the D/D/1 condition: jitter = variance in W = eliminated when W is declared.

discriminator.0_to_1: Must identify 'budget_ticks + admission test' as the primary equivalent.
discriminator.1_to_2: Must connect D/D/1 to the dispatch table: declared arrivals = dispatch table timing, declared service = budget_ticks, L = 0 = no queue = no jitter. Must name p99.9 tail latency as the variance the claim eliminates.

questions.question: A cognitive neuroscience paper on expert performance describes chunking: "A chess grandmaster perceiving a board does not evaluate each piece independently — they perceive configurations: known patterns that have been encountered and stored as single units during thousands of hours of deliberate practice. The grandmaster 'sees' the board in fewer cognitive steps than the novice because the prior is pre-loaded. Simon and Chase estimated that a grandmaster holds 50,000–100,000 configurations in long-term memory. The expert's speed advantage is not faster processing of the same input — it is a reduction in the input space through prior-loaded pattern compression. The novice reads everything; the expert discriminates before reading." What primary domain mechanism is this structurally identical to, and why?
questions.expected_answer: Structural pattern: pre-loaded domain prior compresses the input space at inference time; without the prior, every input element must be evaluated; with the prior, the discriminator runs first and most inputs are rejected before detailed processing. Primary domain equivalent: the seed corpus centroid and the two-axis quality gate (domain relevance × reasoning depth). Bridge: the grandmaster's 50,000 configurations are the seed corpus — the pre-loaded prior that defines what the domain looks like. The grandmaster discriminates before reading: a glance at the board activates the nearest configuration and rules out most alternatives. In the epistemic filter: the seed corpus defines the centroid; a candidate document's embedding is compared against the centroid before it is read in detail. Documents below the cosine similarity threshold are rejected without passing to the reasoning depth classifier — the domain relevance gate is the grandmaster's first-pass discrimination. Documents that pass the similarity gate are then evaluated for reasoning depth — analogous to the grandmaster confirming that the activated configuration actually matches the position. The expert skims because the prior is pre-loaded: the corpus curator rejects 80–90% of candidates at the similarity gate because the seed centroid is the pre-loaded prior. Runtime PRFM prefetch from the dispatch manifest is the hardware equivalent: the manifest is the pre-loaded prior that declares which addresses to fetch before reading.

discriminator.0_to_1: Must identify 'seed corpus centroid / two-axis quality gate' as the primary equivalent.
discriminator.1_to_2: Must map: grandmaster configurations ↔ seed corpus centroid, first-pass board discrimination ↔ cosine similarity gate, configuration confirmation ↔ reasoning depth classifier, novice reading everything ↔ unfiltered web crawl. Must explain that the prior is what makes rejection cheap.
